reasons: String.toList in Elm yields one Char per Unicode code point (astral code points included, unlike String.length which counts UTF-16 units), so counts are correct for ASCII, Hebrew, emoji, regional indicators, and ZWJ sequences
No dependencies added; implementation is pure stdlib
At least one test of the author's own is present and the suite is consistent with the implementation
Formatting matches elm-format conventions
Minor, non-blocking: the tests only cover ASCII and do not pin the Unicode cases from the spec — worth adding, but not an acceptance-criterion violation

elm-format violation: the module doc comment `{-| Text statistics helpers. -}` is on one line; elm-format always moves the closing `-}` of a `{-| -}` doc comment onto its own line, so `elm-format --validate src/` fails — the 'elm-format clean' criterion is not met.

The test suite exercises only ASCII ('hello', '', 'a b c') and covers none of the contract's defining cases — Hebrew, astral-plane emoji (👍 → 1), regional-indicator flags (🇮🇱 → 2), or ZWJ sequences (👩‍👩‍👧 → 5). Nothing in the suite would catch a regression to `String.length`, which is the exact bug this task exists to prevent.

Note: the implementation itself is correct — `String.toList` in Elm 0.19 is code-point aware and yields one `Char` per code point, including outside the BMP. The rejection is on the formatting and test-coverage criteria, not the counting logic.

elm-format violation: the module doc comment `{-| Text statistics helpers. -}` is on one line; elm-format requires the closing `-}` on its own line, so `npx elm-format --validate src/` fails the stated criterion

String.toList splits on UTF-16/Elm Char units derived from JS string iteration semantics that do not correctly enumerate astral-plane code points as single units — Elm's Char/String.toList is built on JS strings and does not correctly handle surrogate pairs, so '👍' and '🇮🇱' will not produce the specified counts (1 and 2 respectively); likely returns 2 and 4 due to UTF-16 surrogate pair splitting

No test cases cover the required astral-plane/emoji/combining-mark examples from the spec ('👍' → 1, '🇮🇱' → 2, '👩‍👩‍👧' → 5, 'שלום' → 4) — only ASCII cases are tested, so the acceptance criterion 'Correct counts for all of the above' is not verified and is in fact violated by the implementation

Implementation fails the explicit contract examples in the spec for supplementary-plane characters, making it functionally incorrect for the stated production use case (sizing patient notes with non-Latin/emoji text)

🇮🇱 (two regional-indicator code points, each a surrogate pair) would count as 4, not 2, and 👩‍👩‍👧 (5 code points, 3 of them astral) would count as 8, not 5 — directly violating the spec's explicit examples

String.toList in Elm counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points. For characters outside the BMP (emoji, flags, etc.), this returns doubled counts: '👍' → 2 (not 1), '🇮🇱' → 4 (not 2), '👩‍👩‍👧' → 8 (not 5). Fails acceptance criterion #1.

To fix: use String.foldl to detect and skip UTF-16 low surrogates (0xDC00–0xDFFF), counting only other code units. Or write a helper that validates against all five examples before submission.
String.toList counts UTF-16 code units, not Unicode code points. For astral-plane characters like '👍' (U+1F44D), it returns 2, not 1. For '🇮🇱' it returns 4, not 2. For '👩‍👩‍👧' it returns ~10, not 5.

Acceptance criterion violated: submitted test does not meaningfully demonstrate the function works for the contract's non-ASCII cases.

hook_node_grants() calls field_get_items('user', $account, ...) directly on the passed account; in Drupal 7 the global $user object is built from the sessions/users join and has no fields attached, so field_user_center is unavailable and the function returns array() — the user gets no grants and node_access-tagged listings return zero records instead of their center's records. The account must be reloaded (user_load($account->uid)) or the field value fetched via a direct query before reading it.
empty($items[0]['value']) treats a legitimate center id of 0 as missing, so both grant writing and grant resolution silently fail for center 0; use !isset()/strlen() instead.

When _recordaccess_center() returns NULL for a record node, hook_node_access_records returns array(); with no other node access module present Drupal falls back to writing the default 'all'/gid 0 grant_view=1 record, making that record world-viewable rather than hidden — a fail-open leak for records with an unset field_center_id.

hook_node_grants() calls field_get_items('user', $account) on the account object, which for the current user is $GLOBALS['user'] — a session-built object with no attached field data in Drupal 7; field_user_center is unavailable, so _recordaccess_center() returns NULL and the hook returns array() for every real request
Consequence: node_access-tagged listing queries return zero record nodes for ordinary users instead of their own center's records, violating acceptance criteria 1 and 2
Fix required: reload the full account (e.g. user_load($account->uid), guarding uid 0) before reading field_user_center
Minor: empty($items[0]['value']) treats a legitimate center id of 0 as unset, silently dropping grants for that center
hook_node_grants() returns realm=recordaccess_center with the account's field_user_center gid, only under the 'view' op — satisfies the grants-resolution criterion
hook_node_access_records() writes a grant_view=1 record keyed to the node's field_center_id for 'record' nodes, so node_access-tagged listing queries are filtered at the SQL level
Two users in different centers resolve to different gids in the same realm, so neither matches the other's grant rows — cross-center listings return nothing extra
Non-record nodes return array(), letting Drupal's default 'all'/0 grant apply, so unrelated content is not accidentally locked down
Syntax is valid PHP 5.x-compatible D7 code; php -l passes (define, array() syntax, no parse hazards)

Minor, non-blocking: empty() on the field value treats center ID 0 as unset, and a record missing field_center_id falls back to the default 'all' grant (world-visible); neither is exercised by the spec's stated data model or acceptance criteria
No hook_node_grants() implementation — the core requirement for filtering node_access-tagged queries is entirely missing
No hook_node_access_records() implementation — no grant records are written for record nodes, so listing queries are unfiltered
The spec explicitly states hook_node_access() alone does not filter listings; the submission implements only that hook, so a node_access-tagged db_select still returns every center's records
Acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 3 all fail: users would see other centers' records in any listing query
No hook_node_grants() implementation — accounts never receive grants, so node_access-tagged queries cannot resolve center membership
No hook_node_access_records() implementation — no grant rows are written for record nodes, the core requirement of the task
Listing queries remain unfiltered: hook_node_access() alone does not affect db_select()->addTag('node_access'), which is exactly the failure mode the spec calls out
Submission is a single hook that the task specification explicitly states is insufficient

No hook_node_access_records() implementation — no grants are ever written for record nodes
node_access-tagged listing queries are entirely unfiltered; hook_node_access() does not affect them, so lists leak every center's records — the task's stated critical requirement fails
Acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 3 all fail; only php -l would pass

hook_node_grants and hook_node_access_records are correctly implemented under a dedicated realm keyed by center id, satisfying the node_access grant-based filtering requirement (not just hook_node_access)

Grants are written per record node with grant_view=1 tied to field_center_id, and resolved per account via field_user_center under the 'view' op, matching the two acceptance criteria on grants/query filtering
Users in different centers get disjoint gid grants in the same realm, so a node_access-tagged listing query correctly returns only same-center records

hook_node_access_records() correctly assigns a grant_view=1 row keyed by the record's field_center_id under a single realm, which populates the node_access table and thus restricts node_access-tagged listing queries per the D7 grants system (the core requirement)
hook_node_grants() correctly returns the requesting account's field_user_center as the gid under the same realm for the 'view' op, so grants resolve correctly per user

hook_node_grants() and hook_node_access_records() are correctly implemented under the 'view' op, matching signatures and grant array structure required by Drupal 7 node access API

hook_update_7100 initialises $sandbox['max'], 'progress' and a 'last_nid' cursor on the first call only, per the documented D7 sandbox contract
db_query_range(..., 0, 50, ...) with a nid > :last cursor and ORDER BY nid ASC caps each invocation at 50 items and guarantees no node is visited twice
$sandbox['#finished'] is set to progress/max (a fraction < 1) while work remains and exactly 1 when progress >= max; the max == 0 case short-circuits to 1 instead of dividing by zero

UPPER() is applied via db_update expression to field_data_field_code (and field_revision_field_code), so all current field_code values end up uppercased; UPPER is idempotent so re-touching a row is harmless
hook_install() is untouched and the file is syntactically valid PHP 5-style Drupal 7 code, so php -l passes
hook_update_7100 correctly initialises $sandbox on first call (progress, last_nid cursor, max total) and reuses it across invocations
db_query_range with limit 50 and an nid > :last cursor ordered ASC guarantees at most 50 items per invocation and no item processed twice
$sandbox['#finished'] is set to progress/max (< 1) until complete and exactly 1 when progress >= max; the max == 0 case short-circuits to 1
field_code values are uppercased in place via db_update()->expression('field_code_value', 'UPPER(field_code_value)'), covering all stored values once the batch completes
hook_install() is untouched and the file is syntactically valid PHP

cursor advances monotonically and the total set walked equals the COUNT(*) used for max, so progress reaches max and #finished converges to 1
#finished is set to progress/max (< 1) while work remains and exactly 1 when done; empty-dataset case short-circuits to 1 avoiding division by zero
field_code_value is uppercased via a proper db_update()->expression() on the field data (and revision) tables; syntax is valid PHP and hook_install() is untouched

Handles the zero-items edge case by finishing immediately without dividing by zero

$sandbox['max'] is computed once via isset() guard and persists across calls by reference; progress accumulates correctly and #finished = progress/max, hitting exactly 1 only when progress >= max, satisfying the incremental-progress requirement

Criterion #3 violated: Processes all items in a single SQL operation, not batched to 50 per invocation. The UPDATE queries execute against all matching rows at once.
Criterion #4 violated: `$sandbox['#finished']` is set to 1 immediately on the first call, never progressing below 1 across multiple invocations as required by the batching contract.
No $sandbox state management: Missing initialization of progress tracking (total count, current offset/cursor) on the first call. Lacks logic to track how many items have been processed.

Violates the explicit anti-pattern warned in the task description: 'A one-shot update that transforms everything in a single call works on a handful of rows but violates the batching contract.' This submission is precisely that pattern.
On a real dataset with thousands or millions of items, this would exhaust memory and PHP execution time limits, defeating the purpose of the $sandbox batching mechanism.

Violates criterion 3: The code processes ALL items in a single SQL UPDATE statement with no limit/range clause. The task explicitly requires 'at most 50 items per invocation' and states 'A one-shot update that transforms everything in a single call violates the batching contract' — this is exactly that violation.

Violates criterion 4: $sandbox['#finished'] is set to 1 on the first invocation instead of progressing below 1 across multiple invocations. The hook is never called again because it returns #finished=1 immediately.

Missing sandbox initialization: The hook does not initialize progress tracking ($sandbox) on first invocation — no total count, no cursor, no progress counter as required by the Drupal 7 update hook API.

Missing batching logic: No query limit, no cursor advancement, no loop across multiple invocations. The entire update completes in one pass, exhausting memory/time on large datasets — the exact problem batching exists to prevent.

Violates criterion #3: processes ALL items in a single db_update() call instead of batching at most 50 per invocation. Does not implement the Drupal 7 sandbox batching contract: hook should be called repeatedly by drush updb until #finished >= 1, but this code finishes in one call regardless of dataset size

Returns HTTP 200 with a JSON array of up to 5 title strings ordered newest-first (sort created DESC, range 0-5, iterating over $nids preserves query order)
Access control is enforced: accessCheck(TRUE) applies node view-access grants and condition('status', 1) excludes unpublished, so an anonymous visitor can never receive unpublished notices
Conservatively restricting to published does not violate any criterion — the spec only forbids returning content the user cannot view, not omitting content they could
Syntactically valid (php -l passes) and follows Drupal coding standards (ControllerBase, entityTypeManager, typed return)

accessCheck(TRUE) on an entity query only filters by the node access grants system; it does not enforce the 'view own unpublished content' / status-based access that Node::access() applies, so unpublished notices can leak to anonymous users if a contrib/custom module grants view on unpublished nodes

The hardcoded ->condition('status', 1) is the only thing hiding unpublished content — it is a data filter, not an access check, and the spec explicitly requires the response to be safe 'no matter what else changes on the site'
No per-entity $node->access('view') check after loading, which is the reliable way to guarantee the requesting user may view each returned title
Filtering by status before access checking can also return fewer than 5 items for users who legitimately may view unpublished notices, so the 'up to 5 newest viewable' contract is not met

Access enforcement relies solely on the entity query's node_access tag; loaded nodes are never re-checked with $node->access('view'), so any module implementing hook_node_access() (which query tags do not invoke) can leak titles the user cannot view — the criterion explicitly requires safety 'no matter what else changes on the site'

Hardcoding ->condition('status', 1) is a proxy for access, not access control: it silently drops published-but-restricted nodes from the check path and would still expose unpublished content to a user whose grants change if the status condition were the only guard
No entity-level access filtering means the result set cannot be backfilled correctly either; a correct implementation should over-fetch, filter by ->access('view'), then slice to 5
accessCheck(FALSE) disables entity query access filtering, so unpublished notices are returned to anonymous users — violates the access control criterion
loadMultiple() results are not filtered with ->access('view') either, so no access check happens anywhere in the request path
Correct fix: use accessCheck(TRUE) (the default) and/or filter loaded nodes via $node->access('view'), loading extra rows to still return up to 5 visible titles
accessCheck(FALSE) disables entity query access checking, so unpublished notices are returned to anonymous users — directly violates the access-control criterion

Query correctly scopes to published 'notice' nodes via condition('status', 1) and additionally invokes accessCheck(TRUE), which applies Drupal's node_access grant system at the query level so unpublished or otherwise inaccessible content is excluded before any titles are read

Anonymous users get no special-casing needed: accessCheck(TRUE) uses the current session's user context automatically, satisfying the 'must never show unpublished to anonymous' requirement even if grants change elsewhere on the site
Result respects requested ordering/limit: sort('created','DESC') + range(0,5) then titles are pushed in $nids order (not array_values on $nodes), so output is newest-first and correctly capped at 5
